one_line_takeaway: BiMarker re-detects the KGW watermark by boosting green logits in one set of token positions and red logits in the other, then testing the green-count difference between the two poles; this cancels the poorly-estimated human baseline and yields up to +20pp TPR at low watermark strength, with the biggest gains when composed with the EWD detector on code.
key_finding: BiMarker's gains are concentrated exactly where KGW is weakest: low watermark strength under multinomial sampling. At delta=1 (multinomial, zero false positives) TPR rises ~10 percentage points over KGW, and up to ~20 points at delta=0.75; in Table 1 (gamma=0.5) at delta=0.5 BiMarker reaches TPR 0.498 vs KGW 0.436 at 1%FPR and 0.744 vs 0.700 at 5%FPR. At high strength the two converge (delta=2.0: 0.994 vs 0.990 at 1%FPR; both hit TPR 1.0 at delta=2.5 under 8-beam search). Composing BiMarker with EWD gives the strongest code-detection result: best-F1 on HumanEval 0.96 (EWD alone 0.869) and MBPP 0.898 (EWD 0.888) at gamma=0.5, delta=2. Mechanistically, z-scores of watermarked text rise while human-text z-scores stay put, widening the separation.
core_contribution: The paper's OWN contribution is BiMarker, a 'bipolar' variant of the KGW logit-biasing watermark for LLM-generated text, together with a matched 'differential' detector. During generation, each token position is pseudo-randomly assigned a polarity (positive with probability rho, negative otherwise): in positive positions the green-list logits are boosted by delta (as in KGW), while in negative positions the RED-list logits are boosted by delta instead. At detection time, instead of comparing the observed green-token count to the fixed reference gamma*T (as KGW does), BiMarker counts green tokens separately in the positive and negative poles and tests their DIFFERENCE, giving z = (|s|_pG - |s|_nG - gamma*Tp + (1-gamma)*Tn) / sqrt(T*gamma*(1-gamma)); when Tp/Tn = (1-gamma)/gamma this simplifies to (|s|_pG - |s|_nG)/sqrt(T*gamma*(1-gamma)). The motivating insight (which the authors frame as a 'new dimension') is that KGW's assumption that non-watermarked text has exactly gamma*T green tokens is inaccurate, especially in low-entropy settings, and that human and watermarked green-token counts move together (empirical Pearson r=0.7); differencing the two poles cancels this shared, inaccurately-estimated baseline. The method adds no inference cost, needs no prompt or auxiliary model, and is presented as orthogonal to entropy-based detectors SWEET and EWD, into which it can be composed.
threat_model: Adversary uses an LLM to produce text and wants it to pass as human-written (e.g. fake news, fabricated academic papers, exam cheating); the defender is the model owner/detector who embedded a logit watermark and must decide watermarked-vs-human. The paper stresses the asymmetric cost of false positives (wrongly accusing a human) and therefore evaluates TPR under very low or zero FPR. A removal/evasion attack is explicitly modeled: back-translation (English->French->English) that edits the text to erase the watermark while preserving meaning (Table 3). Not modeled: watermark forging/spoofing, adaptive attackers who know the polarity rule, or heavy paraphrasing beyond back-translation.
limitations: Reported results are genuinely mixed, not uniformly positive, and the paper is candid about this. On HumanEval at 1%FPR, KGW+BiMarker (TPR 0.352) is actually WORSE than plain KGW (0.375), and SWEET+BiMarker barely moves the needle (0.563 vs 0.561); the clear composition win is only with EWD. Under back-translation at delta=1.5, BiMarker (TPR 0.776 @1%FPR) slightly trails KGW (0.786), so robustness is 'comparable' rather than better. Benefits shrink toward zero as delta grows and under beam search. The core theory assumes positive/negative tokens are independent and identically distributed, which the authors admit is violated by code syntax (adjacent-token dependencies); they mitigate with minimum spacing but note 'watermark integrity risks persist,' and varying token counts prevent hitting the optimal Tp/Tn ratio. Empirical scope is narrow: only OPT-1.3B and StarCoder, one language, one edit-attack, no adaptive adversary aware of the polarity rule, and Theorems 3.1/3.2 are asserted with proofs left to the appendix.

datasets_used: C4 RealNewsLike / news-like subset (high-entropy text generation and human baseline)
quantitative_results: Empirical Pearson correlation of 0.7 between green-token counts in human-written and watermarked texts (motivation study, OPT-1.3B, 500 samples, 200 tokens, gamma=0.5, delta=1)
Table 1 (gamma=0.5, multinomial), delta=0.5: TPR@1%FPR KGW 0.436 vs BiMarker 0.498 (F1 0.603 vs 0.66); TPR@5%FPR KGW 0.700 vs BiMarker 0.744 (F1 0.800 vs 0.829)
Table 1, delta=1.5: TPR@1%FPR KGW 0.978 vs BiMarker 0.986; delta=2.0: 0.990 vs 0.994; beam-search delta=2.0 row: both 0.998 @1%FPR and 1.0 @5%FPR
At delta=1 (multinomial, zero false positives) TPR increases ~10%; at delta=0.75 up to ~20%; under 8-beam search at delta=2.5 both methods reach TPR=1.0
Table 2 code (gamma=0.5, delta=2), HumanEval best-F1: KGW 0.787, KGW+BiMarker 0.797, SWEET 0.875, SWEET+BiMarker 0.872, EWD 0.869, EWD+BiMarker 0.96
Table 2 MBPP best-F1: KGW 0.739, KGW+BiMarker 0.749, SWEET 0.85, SWEET+BiMarker 0.852, EWD 0.888, EWD+BiMarker 0.898
Table 2 HumanEval TPR@1%FPR: KGW 0.375 vs KGW+BiMarker 0.352 (BiMarker slightly worse); SWEET 0.561 vs 0.563; EWD 0.609 vs EWD+BiMarker 0.671
Table 3 back-translation attack (gamma=0.5), delta=0.5: TPR@1%FPR KGW 0.188 vs BiMarker 0.272; delta=1.5: KGW 0.786 vs BiMarker 0.776 (comparable)
Detection setup: 500 samples per config, ~200 +/- 5 tokens; code samples restricted to >=15 tokens
baselines_compared: KGW (Kirchenbauer et al. 2023a) — primary baseline logit watermark
SWEET (Lee et al. 2024) — entropy-thresholded KGW detector for code
EWD (Lu et al. 2024) — entropy-weighted KGW detector (described as current SOTA for low-entropy)
